description: Cummins is a global power-systems manufacturer spanning engines, components, distribution, power generation, and zero-emissions technologies through Accelera by Cummins.

overview.title: Business Profile
paragraphs: Cummins designs, manufactures, distributes, and services powertrain and power generation products for commercial, industrial, mining, marine, rail, construction, agricultural, and defense markets. The company is still strongly associated with diesel engines, but its actual footprint is broader: engines, aftertreatment, turbochargers, filtration, fuel systems, transmissions, generators, controls, and service networks.

Its operating structure is organized around five segments: Engine, Components, Distribution, Power Systems, and Accelera by Cummins. That mix gives Cummins a durable position in hard-to-electrify markets where customers tend to value uptime, torque, and service coverage more than elegant investor-deck vocabulary.

overview.title: Product And Platform Map
paragraphs: The core Cummins product family centers on diesel, natural gas, and alternative-fuel engines for medium-duty and heavy-duty applications, plus generator sets and power systems for standby, prime, and continuous power. Components extend the company deeper into the machinery stack through emissions control, air handling, drivetrain, braking, electronics, and related systems.

Accelera by Cummins houses the company’s zero-emissions technology portfolio, including battery systems, fuel cells, ePowertrain systems, and electrolyzers. It is strategically important for the registry because it marks Cummins’ attempt to bridge from incumbent combustion infrastructure into cleaner power systems without pretending the installed base will politely vanish on schedule.

overview.title: Strategic Context
paragraphs: Cummins sits at the center of several transition tensions: freight and industrial customers need lower emissions, but many still operate in duty cycles where battery-electric replacement is technically or economically uneven. The company’s Destination Zero framing positions it as a transition supplier across advanced diesel, natural gas, hydrogen, batteries, fuel cells, and power generation.

Power generation has become especially relevant as data centers, grid constraints, and resilience planning increase demand for backup and distributed power. This creates a useful registry contrast: Cummins can enable both fossil-dependent backup infrastructure and lower-emissions systems, sometimes in the same corporate sentence, which is convenient for everyone except anyone seeking simple labels.
